speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about how you left Israel and married this wonderful mister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told him, you are nuts. You are married, I am married. He said, you have never heard of divorce? So I said, yes, I've heard about it, but I am not ready yet. Think it over. I tell you, he said-- he was a very interesting guy, very good looking. I am a Capricorn. I get always what I want. Sometimes it takes time, but I get you too. I tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we separated. I went with my husband to Israel, and he went to America. And he made phone calls to Israel and he said, I kept my word. I separated already from my wife. How far are you? I said, I have done nothing. First of all, my husband is against divorce. And after Jewish law, when a husband says no, it's no. So I said, we think about it. I was not too keen. He was 24 years older than I was. So I thought 24 years is-- even in old age, a very, very big difference, yes? I had felt already with my first husband that 14 years were a lot. But we were still young.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this guy, even when he was 24 years older, you had not an impression that he's a very old man. Anyway, I lived in Israel and went to Europe. And this doctor had a beautiful baritone voice. Because in Berlin, he was a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband? Leopold Goldberg. And now I go speaking of Goldberg. I go mostly under Kimmel. Do you know why? In America, when I say Goldberg, everyone say, are you related to Whoopi? And then I have always to say, only by name! And so I say, mostly, my name is Kimmel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, he sent me his records to Israel, or to Switzerland, wherever I was, this Dr. Goldberg. And my husband was very angry. He said, this stupid old guy of San Francisco, he doesn't give up! I said, what does it hurt you? We are still married. What do you want? So we were listening to the voice. He had a beautiful voice. Do you want a few-- a few tones? Would that be nice? Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? I could do it possibly. Set you here. He could sing. I don't think-- without my glasses, I don't see. Plus he could sing in French and Italian, in German. Whatever you want. No? Why doesn't it work? [MUSIC PLAYING] [SINGING] You tell me where to stop. [SINGING] I love thee more than any other. I love thee. Stop? Or less volume? Wait one moment. [SINGING] I love thee now and for eternity. I have to explain. This is without the gadgets of today. That was recorded when he was already a very old man, and he-- I think he was nearly 85 when he was singing this. But it was without the gadgets, and the very bad, how should I say? He never spent money on himself. I think he took the cheapest place to have it recorded, and so it sounds. And so we lived our separate lives. And '79, my husband passed away. '79 or '78? My calculator here I thinks needs the batteries recharged.

speaker: interviewee
text: '78, yeah. And so he knew from other friends, my husband passed away. So he sent me immediately an air ticket to San Francisco. And I sent it immediately back. [LAUGHS].

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, was I too early? I said, yes, you were too early. And when I come to San Francisco, I don't want any obligation. I come on my own ticket and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one year later, I went really to San Francisco. I have to tell you, I knew San Francisco. I had a trip around the world, '52. And fell in love with San Francisco, not in connection with Leopold Goldberg. I haven't met him. But I liked San Francisco. And I said, I'm coming back in old age. And I kept my word.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I sent him the picture back, his ticket back. And in the meantime, my daughter had a beautiful condominium in Rome. A beautiful, a penthouse. Very, very expensive. And she rarely spent half a year, or only three months, possibly, there. So she came and she said, do you know? We are both lonely cats. You have here this house that you practically use half a year. I use Rome three months. Let's sell and buy some property together in America. Because I think the American dollar will be the last currency that will be changed to rubles. She still thought-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: She still thought, said Russia were the power ones. Now her opinion is very, very changed. Anyway, we both decided to sell. And I came to America, really, to have a look if I would like to live here for good. And so I met Dr. Goldberg again. And I was still attracted. But I said, I don't want to get married, you see. You as so much older than I am. Let's live together, and let's see. He said, no. I don't live in sin. Anyway, I didn't marry him so quick. I went to-- I found a place in San Rafael that my daughter and I liked, a very large house in a very good neighborhood, and we decided to buy it. And I have said-- I go to Israel to liquidate, and to liquidate in Rome. That's what we did, and we both did, and I came back to America, and we lived in this house together in San Rafael. I had brought furniture that I had. Good ones, still. Good ones. And she brought furniture, and the house was beautifully furnished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had with her an agreement. We live together, yeah? And it-- you see, my daughter and I, we have only an age difference of 19 years. So that is not another generation. So we both thought it would work out. And it didn't work out. First of all, she went to Paris, and I was in this large building with four bedrooms, four bathrooms. You have no idea where-- I felt lonely. And every night came the deers down. We head in the backyard a mountain, a swimming pool. The deers came down, and we had a sensible insurance. And the insurance went on, and the sheriff came every night. And it was like in an old Hollywood movie, yeah? I couldn't sleep anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to her, I said, it's out for me. I can't do it. It takes my nerves, because when you live here, yeah, it's all right. But you live in Paris and give me orders what to do. And then come another thing. We had some financial trouble. She has not brought everything what she promised, and I had it, you see. So practically all my-- what I had was in this building. And it had troubles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Leopold Goldberg came to visit us. And he said, your mother doesn't want to get married. What should I do? She said, that's your problem. You do what you want. He said, but your mother is nervous. She has all her money invested in this house. You know it.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, yes. You can help her out. You have man. He said, no. I mentioned her in my last will, anyway. She said-- I thought it was a very tactless remark, so you give her something what you don't use anymore anyway. But he laughed it. He liked it very much. He said, she's-- he liked her too, and she says what she thinks. Anyway, I was not very happy in San Rafael. And I took my dog and my suitcases, and I went to San Francisco. And I stayed with him, and we said we'd try it out. But he influenced me, and we got married. And I'm sorry for every day that I have not married him before. Because he was a very interesting man, even when he was an old man, and it was not-- I was not bored in his company one moment. And he was the most easygoing, adoring husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I still remember-- you see, my first husband took everything that I did for granted. And he was grateful for everything. When we said good night, he would say every day, goodnight, Liebchen. Liebchen is like 'darling,' yeah? Goodnight, I thank you for a beautiful day. And this gives a lot to a woman. And even when he fell sick-- the last year was very difficult. I had him in three nursing homes. And in three nursing homes, I couldn't see how he was treated. And I spoke with his treating doctor, and said, I take Leopold out. He said, you can't do it. I said, what do you mean I can't do it? He's 100 years old. He was already 100, yeah? No, nearly 100. A few months left. He said, I don't speak for him. You can't do it. You wouldn't be able to do it. I said, I tell you something. The last nursing home is not so bad. Not the worst. I pay his room and make an agreement that he can come back. I pay the room, yeah? All the room. That is not the end of the world. And I shall try how it works at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, Johanna, you are quite a lady. And I took him home, and I had three nurses and a woman for cleaning, because it's very bad that I'm married to a cook, but I'm not married to a house cleaner. This is would be the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, he gained weight, and he was perfect at home. And he celebrated here in the building his 100th birthday. And he insisted on music. I mean, live music. And it was beautiful. And I tell you, he was the most vain person I know. He made a dress rehearsal with the nurses if he should wear a tie or a turtleneck. And in the end, he said, we'll let Mrs. decide. And we had a beautiful birthday. And he passed away approximately six-- one months after his birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we talk about the Shoah, or the Holocaust, again? It's 50 years after the Shoah. Do you think about it often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very often in Germany after this, and very often in Austria. It's hard to say. The people that I'm meeting now are mostly children of-- or grandchildren of people who-- were really the devils in the Holocaust. You don't meet them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what I thought most disgusting, when they are so very humble, you see? Do you understand what I mean? That is worse than the contrary. But I can't say. I have a feeling there is still anti-Semitism, of course, more in Austria than perhaps in Germany. You feel it more. I was reading in a newspaper, in Austria, there was a question of hundred people, would you shake hands with a Jew? And 80 said no. That is a very sad sign still. But I hope still-- you see, I have letters from Australia, yeah? I will show it to you later. Where they say-- they offered me a small sum, yeah? As a kind of-- not [NON-ENGLISH] because they say, we can't repay the sorrow that we brought to you. The loss of health, the loss of your homeland. The loss of personal relationships, we can't, or property.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we try. We try-- here, this letter of the government. We try to make a bridge, be helpful. This is the answer. But one tries, you see. Possibly, I met one honest person. Perhaps there are more. Perhaps they regret. I don't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: In conclusion, Mrs. Goldberg, is there any message you'd like to leave for your children or for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean in America?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anywhere in the world. About Holocaust, about survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I say, [INAUDIBLE]. It shouldn't happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much, Mrs. Goldberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: The pleasure was mine. I wouldn't say it was a pleasure, because you see, the pictures are coming back, and I hope I shall sleep tonight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that we've come to the end of our interview, I'd like you to present us with some photographs and documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to introduce you to my mother, Charlotte Schmiedl, [NON-ENGLISH]. A liberated woman. I'll explain. And who was so very Victorian, and changed her mind, and was very happy to be in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your mother there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, when she passed away, was 78.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this picture, how old is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is your mother in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately 76. This is Richard Joseph Schmiedl, a proud Austrian. A proud Viennese citizen. And this is picture during the war. He was an Austrian soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which war, Mrs. Goldberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died. He passed away '31.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which war was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the First World War. He passed away '31. This is my first husband, Ignaz [NON-ENGLISH] Kimmel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was made in Berlin, when we were not-- we were nearly engaged, so he sent me this picture. That was '30, 1930. This was my daughter at the age of 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was done in Vienna. And this is my daughter today. Her name is Helga. She's born October 31. And she lives in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and this is my daughter. This is me, Christmas '29. I was very proud. I got my first fur coat. I was 17 years old. This is me at 38, when I left Vienna. That was because the immigration, my last photo in Vienna. In this time, I had black hair. And in Israel, on my 50th birthday, I decided suddenly, whenever I look in the mirror, it's dark. Now I'm a blonde. And so I changed my hair. My father, top left, in the Austrian army. He was a very good looking man. And the women were always running after him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the date on this picture, please? Do you know what date it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was during the war, you see? I'm born '12, and the war was '14, I can't tell you very much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'll tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my second husband, Leopold Goldberg, on the day when we decided to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the date. I don't know. I remember when we got married. It was 4th of August, '81. But I don't know exactly the date when this picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is from different trips my husband and I made. He was a very attractive man, even when he was 90. And we made-- I remember, one trip, we went to Lake Chapala in Mexico on continued education. And there were only doctors. And they say to him, listen, Leopold, are you not jealous? Your wife is so much younger and attractive, and see everyone is flirting with her! He said, I would never have wanted a wife that no one else wants.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a birth certificate of my daughter, Helga. In Vienna, October 15, '31. This is [NON-ENGLISH]. That means the release forms of the concentration camp in Buchenwald, when I got him back. It was September '38, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my birth certificate. I was born May 31, 1912. In Vienna. That's a marriage certificate from [INAUDIBLE], when I married my first husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 9th, '30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name? Your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ignaz Bob Kimmel. The letters of the Nationalfonds der Republik Osterreich, fur Opfer des Nationalsozialiismus. And they offered me an amount of 70,000 shillings. That is approximately $6,000. And they say they know perfectly well that this small amount is no equivalent for that what I've suffered. And they don't think it is a [NON-ENGLISH], as you say in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Restitution.

speaker: interviewee
text: It can't take out the sorrow and the loss of a homeland, the loss of health, the loss of a whole life. These are letters of admission of the Austrian government, what they really did to the Jews. They say they hope, with this small amount they're sending, they can let us forget what we suffered in the darkest moments of German history. This short letter is the same thing, more or less. And they hope, with a small amount, to make a bridge with the Jews and the Austrian government. Because they say they know what they did, and they are awfully sorry. And they know the saying sorry doesn't help a little bit. This is a interview that was taken from my husband, from the San Francisco Chronicle or Examiner, I don't know. For outstanding citizens of San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Leopold Goldberg, MD. This is in memoriam of my late husband from the CMA, the California Medical Association. Leopold Goldberg, believed to be the oldest practicing doctor in San Francisco. When he retired, at age 95, died of pneumonia, he was 100 years old. A native of Posen, Germany, he was a certain child of poor parents. Possessed of a fine baritone voice, he earned enough singing in cafes and small opera companies to enter the University of Berlin. Years later, in [INAUDIBLE], a patient urged him to forget medicine for the opera. Dr. Goldberg earned his medical degree 1919 at Heidelberg, where he later taught. He was later invited in Heidelberg University, because he was the only living person who graduated and lectured there. During the war, he served in the German army as a medical officer and was decorated with the iron cross first class. He practiced in Berlin, and in 1937, when he fled Germany to escape the regime of Adolf Hitler. Arriving in San Francisco, he faced the enormous problem of finding an internship in order to become licensed. Fortunately, a doctor-- a local doctor, WB [INAUDIBLE], recognized Dr. Goldberg as one of his lecturers in Heidelberg and got him an interim position at Southern Pacific Hospital at the prevailing rate of $15 per month. Once licensed, Dr. Goldberg divided his time between private offices in San Francisco and Stockton. At age 65, when most workers are considering retirement, he went to Europe for 18 months, post-graduate training in geriatrics medicine, returned to the city to practice his speciality for another 25 years. [INAUDIBLE] was a remarkable man in the renaissance sense. Both as a physician and as an ever interested observer of humanity. He drove, however-- he drove his own car until '96. He remained [INAUDIBLE] instead in medicine, music, opera, cultural events, travel and [INAUDIBLE] until his final illness. He is survived by his wife, Johanna, and his daughter, [INAUDIBLE] Roberts, who share their society's sorrow
